text: During laparoscopic anterior resection, near-infrared fluorescence highlights the left ureter, which appears visibly enhanced after retrograde injection of indocyanin green through a catheter.

The surgeon activates the switch on the Davinci XI system to alternate between standard white light and near-infrared views. The indocyanine green dye fills the ureter, emitting a fluorescent green signal that clearly indicates the ureter's position during the laparoscopic anterior resection.

The catheter is positioned externally and connected to a syringe, enabling the surgeon to boost the indocyanine green signal if it fades, and facilitating easy removal at the end of the procedure to minimize patient discomfort.

Ureteric injury during surgery has a reported incidence of approximately one percent and it's particularly likely in patients who are obese or have had prior inflammatory conditions in their abdomen including prior operation and diverticulitis. The da Vinci XI system contains Firefly capability built in allowing this switching between the dual illumination modalities in order to provide easy recognition and safety of critical structures. In this particular case, the patient had a BMI of thirty-one and had prior recurrent attacks of diparticulitis needing hospital admission on six occasions in the previous one year.
